1: "Praise the LORD! I will thank the LORD with my whole heart, in the gathering of the upright, in the full congregation" — the wholehearted praise that opens this acrostic psalm. Hebrews 2:12 quotes Psalms 22:22 in Christ's voice: "I will declare your name to my brothers and sisters; in the assembly I will sing your praises." The gathered congregation praising YHWH is the church gathered around the risen Christ, the one who leads the praise in the midst of his brothers. The wholehearted thanksgiving of Psalms 111:1 is Christ's own song in the assembly.
2: "The works of the LORD are great, studied carefully by all who find pleasure in them" — the contemplation of divine works as the task of the godly community. Christ is both the great work of God and the one who reveals it: "The Son can do nothing by himself; he can only do what he sees his Father doing, because whatever the Father does the Son also does" (John 5:19). The works studied with delight are ultimately the works of redemption accomplished in Christ — the disciples who beheld his signs "believed in him" (John 2:11).
3: "All he does is glorious and majestic, and his righteousness endures forever" — the permanence of divine righteousness expressed in his works. The righteousness that endures forever is the righteousness revealed in the gospel: "For in the gospel the righteousness of God is revealed — a righteousness that is by faith from first to last" (Romans 1:17). The enduring righteousness of YHWH's works reaches its definitive expression in the righteous act of Christ's atoning death (Romans 5:18: "just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people").
4: "He has made his wonders unforgettable; the LORD is gracious and full of compassion" — the hannun werahhum formula from Exodus 34:6, the great self-revelation of YHWH to Moses at Sinai. John 1:14 makes the connection explicit: "The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us. We have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son, who came from the Father, full of grace and truth." The grace-and-compassion of YHWH's wonders is the grace-and-truth that the disciples beheld in the person of Christ — the Sinai formula fulfilled in the Incarnation.
5: "He provides food for all who fear him; he is always mindful of his covenant" — the feeding-God who keeps covenant. Jesus feeds five thousand (John 6:1-13) and then explains: "I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty" (John 6:35). The feeding and covenant-mindfulness of Psalms 111:5 are fulfilled together in Christ who is simultaneously the new covenant's mediator and the bread that sustains covenant life.
6: "He showed his people what his works can do, giving them the nations' land as their possession" — the power of divine works in land-grant. Hebrews 4:8-9: "For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken later about another day. There remains, then, a Sabbath-rest for the people of God." The land-possession that Joshua achieved was provisional; the true land-grant is the eschatological inheritance secured by Christ — "the meek will inherit the earth" (Matthew 5:5), the whole renewed creation.
7: "Everything he does is faithful and just; all his instructions are reliable" — the reliable character of divine action and teaching. Jesus says "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6) — the reliability of divine instructions reaches its embodied form in Christ who is himself truth. Revelation 3:14 calls Christ "the Amen, the faithful and true witness" — the faithful-and-just character of Psalms 111:7 is the personal identity of Christ the Witness.
8: "They hold firm forever and ever, carried out with complete faithfulness and integrity" — the permanence of divine instructions. Hebrews 13:8: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever." Matthew 5:18: "until heaven and earth disappear, not the smallest letter, not the least stroke of a pen, will by any means disappear from the Law until everything is accomplished." The holding-firm-forever of divine ordinances is the permanence of Christ who fulfills every one of them and holds them together in his own eternal being.
9: "He sent redemption (pedut) to his people and established his covenant forever. His name is holy and full of awe" — two of the most direct Messianic categories united in one verse. Hebrews 9:12: "he entered once for all into the Most Holy Place, not by means of the blood of goats and calves, but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption." Hebrews 13:20: "the God of peace, who brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the eternal covenant." The redemption sent and the covenant established forever in Psalms 111:9 are the precise terms Hebrews uses to describe what Christ accomplishes — eternal redemption through eternal covenant blood.
10: "The fear of the LORD is where wisdom begins; those who live by it gain deep understanding. His praise endures forever" — the concluding wisdom-saying. First Corinthians Psalms 1:24: "Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God." Colossians 2:3: "in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge." The fear of the LORD that begins wisdom is, in Christ, the reverence before the one who is Wisdom incarnate. The praise that endures forever is the eternal worship of the Lamb (Revelation 5:13).
